perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "Proprietary Information" means any confidential, non-public or proprietary information, technology or documentation, including without limitation, drawings, designs, business plans and proposals, ideas and prototypes that may be disclosed by STI to Pinetree, whether such is of a technical, commercial, organizational, scientific, financial or business nature.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term "Proprietary Information" means any information, whether confidential, public, or previously known, including drawings, designs, business plans and proposals, ideas and prototypes that may be disclosed by STI to Pinetree, whether such is of a technical, commercial, organizational, scientific, financial or business nature.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of Proprietary Information is altered to include public and previously known information, contradicting the standard legal understanding of trade secrets and confidential information. This creates a contradiction within the contract regarding what information is actually protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. The modified text contradicts this definition by including any information, even if publicly available, as 'Proprietary Information,' making the agreement's protection of such information legally questionable under Maryland law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201 (2022) - Definitions :: 2022 Maryland Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Maryland Code 2023 Maryland Code 2022 Maryland Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Maryland Statutes Commercial Law Title 11 - Trade Regulation Subtitle 12 - Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 11-1201 - Definitions Universal Citation: MD Commercial Law Code § 11-1201 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Next (a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (c) “Misappropriation” means the: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person’s knowledge of the trade secret was: 1. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; 2. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of the person’s position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (d) “Person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, statutory trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or a
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "Proprietary Information" to include publicly available information. This directly contradicts the legal understanding of trade secrets, which, according to Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201, derives its value from *not* being generally known. By including public information, the modified contract weakens any potential legal protection for genuine trade secrets, as it attempts to protect information that does not meet the statutory definition.

perturbation.original_text: Unless a particular portion of Proprietary Information becomes nonproprietary as provided in Section 4(a) hereof, or unless otherwise authorized in a written agreement signed by an authorized officer of STI, Pinetree has a duty to protect each particular portion of Proprietary Information in for a period of five (5) years from the date of this Agreement, provided that Proprietary Information which constitutes a trade secret under applicable law shall be protected as long as the Proprietary Information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a particular portion of Proprietary Information becomes nonproprietary as provided in Section 4(a) hereof, or unless otherwise authorized in a written agreement signed by an authorized officer of STI, Pinetree has a duty to protect each particular portion of Proprietary Information for a period of one (1) year from the date of this Agreement, regardless of whether Proprietary Information constitutes a trade secret under applicable law.
perturbation.explanation: The duration of protection for trade secrets is limited to one year, regardless of whether the information qualifies as a trade secret under Maryland law. This directly contradicts the legal principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide economic value.
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting the protection of trade secrets to one year, the modified text directly conflicts with the Maryland Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which provides for ongoing protection as long as the information meets the definition of a trade secret, regardless of a fixed time period.

Next (a) In this subtitle the following words have the meanings indicated. (b) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (c) “Misappropriation” means the: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the person’s knowledge of the trade secret was: 1. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; 2. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of the person’s position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (d) “Person” means an individual, corporation, business trust, statutory trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or a
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the protection of trade secrets to one year, directly contradicting the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as they remain secret and provide economic value, which is the core of trade secret law. The original text acknowledges that trade secrets under applicable law should be protected as long as they qualify as such under Maryland law. The modified text ignores this and sets an arbitrary one-year limit, which means that after one year, the information is no longer protected regardless of its trade secret status. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Maryland Commercial Law Code Section 11-1201, defines 'Misappropriation' which involves the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent.' By limiting the protection to one year, the modified text effectively allows for the disclosure or use of trade secrets after that period, even if they still qualify as trade secrets, which is a direct contradiction to the legal definition of misappropriation and the intent of trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: Pinetree must protect the Proprietary Information from unauthorized use or disclosure by exercising the same degree of care that Pinetree uses with respect to information of its own of a similar, highly valuable nature, and Pinetree must, at minimum, use at least reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Pinetree must protect the Proprietary Information from unauthorized use or disclosure by exercising the same degree of care that Pinetree deems necessary, and Pinetree may, at minimum, use no care at all.
perturbation.explanation: The standard of care required to protect the Proprietary Information is lowered to 'no care at all,' directly contradicting the duty of care normally associated with protecting confidential information and trade secrets. This creates a situation where Pinetree has no real obligation to safeguard the information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a statutory definition, the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contract law requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of a contract. Allowing Pinetree to exercise 'no care at all' in protecting Proprietary Information violates this duty, as it effectively undermines the purpose of the Non-Disclosure Agreement. While Maryland has not adopted a universal duty of good faith, depending on the context of the contract, this can be applied.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the obligation of good faith by allowing Pinetree to exercise 'no care at all' in protecting Proprietary Information. This undermines the purpose of the Non-Disclosure Agreement, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The modified text removes any obligation of care, thus violating this principle, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: STI may take any necessary action to compel specific performance of this Agreement or to enjoin any violation of this Agreement so that Proprietary Material is not used in any manner to harm or adversely affect STI.
perturbation.changed_text: STI may only take action to compel specific performance of this Agreement if Pinetree intentionally and maliciously uses Proprietary Material to directly cause the financial ruin of STI. No other violations will be subject to legal recourse.
perturbation.explanation: The conditions under which STI can seek specific performance or injunctive relief are severely restricted, essentially nullifying the enforceability of the agreement for any violation short of intentional, malicious financial ruin. This is a significant departure from the typical remedies available for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Remedies for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification limits the availability of specific performance, a standard remedy for breach of contract, far beyond what is typically allowed under contract law. The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance will not be granted if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party's interests. The modified text implies that only near-total financial ruin justifies specific performance, which is an unreasonable restriction on STI's rights and contradicts established principles of contract law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text severely restricts STI's ability to seek specific performance, limiting it to cases of intentional, malicious financial ruin. This directly contradicts the original agreement, which allowed STI to take action to compel performance or prevent any violation that harms STI's proprietary material. The Law Explanation states this alteration limits the availability of specific performance, a standard remedy, far beyond what is typically allowed, which suggests that the modification is an unreasonable restriction on STI's rights and contradicts established principles of contract law.
